Ronald Williams stepped down as CEO amidst the transformation of federal health-care law in the middle of the Great Recession. He assumed the role of Executive Chairman of Aetna, Inc. before completely retiring from the company in 2011. His successor, Mr. Bertolini, headed the corporation's lobbying efforts with governors and regulators. Seeing as how Aetna's market came primarily from selling insurance to large corporate entities, the shareholders probably decided that the legislative overhaul was best dealt with by an insider in Washington politics. Still not too old at sixty years of age, it is unlikely that Mr. William's retirement was voluntary given the circumstances. His salary was relatively generous at $85 million in five years (appx. $17 million a year), but the company's stocked dropped by a third of what it was at the beginning of Mr. William's term. Profitability also declined from $1.7 billion to $1.3 billion over the same period to boot.
  
  Changed code to 5. Ronald Williams was hired in 2001 and has since turned around the health insurer from an ailing company to a profitable one. He has retired after helping the company to improve its financial condition as well as navigate through the federal healthcare overhaul process. His successor, Mark Bertolini, has worked closely with state governors during this overahaul process while he was laisioning with policymakers at Washington. Mark Bertoloni would thus be suited to lead the company under a new healthcare policy regime.

- “The company has underperformed, and they haven’t delivered on a lot of things,” said Brian Tanquilut, a Jefferies analyst. “From a board perspective, they probably felt like a change could help bridge the gap in valuation between Cardinal and its peers.”

Cardinal Health’s board of directors has selected chief financial officer Jason Hollar to be the healthcare distributor’s new CEO, the company said Thursday.

Hollar will begin serving as the company’s top executive on Sept. 1. Current CEO Mike Kaufmann, who has led Cardinal Health for the past five years and has worked within the company for more than three decades, will step down.

Hollar joined Cardinal as CFO in 2020 and in that time he “has helped Cardinal Health prioritize investments in growth businesses, strengthened the balance sheet, and returned capital to shareholders,” the company said in a statement.
Prior to Cardinal, Hollar served in the CFO role at automotive components company, Tenneco, and retail company Sears Holdings Corporation.
Patricia English will become Cardinal’s interim CFO.

Watson's successor has not been named, but the leading candidate to replace Watson, 60, is refining specialist Michael Wirth, 56, according to sources. Wirth was promoted to vice chairman of Chevron earlier this year. 

Watson's departure from the company is amicable, according to the Journal, even though he is retiring well before the company's mandatory retirement age.

CINCINNATI--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ: FITB) announced today that Greg Carmichael has elected to retire as chief executive officer effective July 5. At that time, Carmichael will become executive chairman, continuing to lead the Board of Directors and provide advice and counsel to the CEO. Tim Spence will succeed Carmichael as Fifth Third’s CEO.

Credit: Aaron Conway
Credit: Aaron Conway

Marsha Williams, lead independent director of Fifth Third’s Board, expressed the Board’s unanimous confidence in Spence. “Tim is an outstanding leader who will continue to build on the Bank’s strong momentum, which he has helped create. He has been an integral part of the leadership team since 2015, helping develop the strategies that Fifth Third is executing with excellence through innovation, technology and citizenship. Tim has brought resilience throughout the pandemic that has been instrumental in propelling Fifth Third to the forefront of customer-centric, digital-first banking, enabling our employees to serve the Bank’s customers during their time of greatest need.”

“I am honored to serve as Fifth Third’s next CEO and humbled to follow in the footsteps of an incredible leader like Greg,” said Spence. “Fifth Third is a great company because of our people, because we serve strong communities, and because we believe to the core that our customers’ needs, their opportunities and their well-being are our chief concern. We will continue our track record of delivering on our commitments, our focus on investing for the future and our culture of accountability, collaboration and community leadership.”

Mr. Light resigned as C.E.O. of Signet Jewelers Ltd., citing health concerns amidst a slew of hospitalizations and surgeries. However, a 69,000 woman-strong class-action lawsuit filed against the company alleged that Mr. Light, among other executives, discriminated against female employees. Claims of discrimination in pay and promotion practices featured in the lawsuit, along with accusations of sexual harassment. The company naturally denied all claims of impropriety and continues to do so presently. In Jock v. Sterling Jewelers, Inc., the company successfully enforced arbitration but with unexpected results. The arbitrator certified a class of 44,000 female employees in 2015, not distinguishing in the process between those who made accusations and vice versa. Sterling motioned to vacate the determination, arguing that the arbitrator exceeded her powers. Judge Jed S. Rakoff upheld Sterling's motion in Jan. 2018. The plaintiffs immediately appealed the decision to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second District. On 18 Nov. 2019, the court reversed and remanded the case back to trial court. The opinion stated that since all employees signed arbitration agreements with the employer, they are accordingly bound by the arbitrator's definition of class. The court is now considering whether the arbitrator has the power to certify an opt-out, instead of a mandatory, class. A brief description of Jock v. Sterling Jewelers, Inc. is included by me because the current case is directly connected to Mr. Light's departure. Since arbitration is still in limbo, no judgment has been ruled. Ergo, Mr. Light's claims of ill health must be accepted as it was far more of an immediate concern than the developing case aforementioned. Attached is the U.R.L. to the legal summary of Jock v. Sterling Jewelers, Inc.: https://www.natlawreview.com/article/second-circuit-affirms-expansion-gender-bias-class-action-against-sterling-jewelers

Iron Mountain Incorporated (NYSE: IRM), the information storage and management company, today announced that its Board of Directors has voted unanimously to appoint William Meaney President and Chief Executive Officer and a member of the Board, effective Jan. 7, 2013. He will succeed longtime Executive Chairman and CEO Richard Reese, who announced his intention to retire after 31 years at the Company.

William Meaney, 52, will become president and CEO of information storage and management company Iron Mountain on Jan. 7, 2013. (Photo: Business Wire)

Meaney, 52, comes to Iron Mountain with more than 20 years of experience successfully overseeing diverse businesses in the United States, Europe, Asia and Africa. His track record over this time reveals a proven ability to drive growth even in challenging environments and maximize returns on investment in capital intensive businesses. Most recently, Meaney served as CEO of the Hong Kong-based Zuellig Group, a $12 billion, primarily business-to-business conglomerate that saw sales triple during his eight-year tenure from 2004-2012.

Prior to leading The Zuellig Group, Meaney spent five years in the airline industry, serving as the Chief Commercial Officer and Managing Director of publicly-traded Swiss International Airlines from December 2002 to January 2004 and Executive Vice President of South African Airways from 1998 to 2001. Meaney helped lead both airlines through challenging times and eventual turnarounds that netted higher profitability and improved returns on capital.

Earlier in his career, Meaney served as the acting CEO of South African Vaccine Producers; the Founder and Managing Director of Genhro Management Consultancy; and a principal of Strategic Planning Associates, now part of the Oliver Wyman Group. Meaney currently serves on the board of Qantas Airways Limited. He is a trustee of both Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute and Carnegie Mellon University, where he earned his bachelor's in mechanical engineering and a master's in industrial administration, respectively.

Commenting on his appointment, Meaney said, "This is an exciting opportunity for me. Iron Mountain is a global market leader with a compelling customer value proposition and a large market opportunity. What's more, the Company is executing on a strong financial strategy to drive returns on invested capital and return excess capital to shareholders that I believe is right for both the business and investors. I look forward to working with the Board, management team, and our talented employees to deliver outstanding services to our customers and drive value for shareholders."

On September 4, 2019, Tapestry, Inc. announced that Jide Zeitlin, the Company’s Chairman of the Board, has been appointed Chief Executive Officer. Mr. Zeitlin succeeds Victor Luis, who is leaving the Company and its Board of Directors. Mr. Zeitlin will continue in his role as Chairman. All appointments are effective immediately. An internal email seen by Vogue Business confirms that Luis was removed after poor performance at Kate Spade and Stuart Weitzman, Tapestry’s two smaller brands. “Executing in [a volatile retail environment] and integrating two new and incredible brands has not always been as smooth as we would hope and we have not met our expectations in delivering shareholder value,” wrote Zeitlin in an email to Tapestry employees. “Given we have not delivered on our potential, the Board decided it was time to make a change at the top and asked me to step in as CEO”.

On July 21, 2020, the Tapestry, Inc. announced that Jide Zeitlin, the company’s Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, resigned from the Company and resigned from the Company’s Board of Directors, effective as of July 20, 2020. Tapestry said that Joanne Crevoiserat, its chief financial officer, would serve as interim chief executive and that it had started a search for a permanent replacement. Jide Zeitlin later said the unexpected move, less than a year into his tenure, was related to a past relationship. The company’s board had been made aware of a misconduct allegation involving Mr. Zeitlin and hired a law firm to investigate, according to a person familiar with the situation who spoke on the condition of anonymity. “I reached the conclusion this past weekend that this distraction will not allow me to meet my responsibilities as C.E.O.,” Mr. Zeitlin wrote in a LinkedIn post.
